title: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases

items.subtopic: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases | SOFT TISSUE SARCOMAS | INCIDENCE | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Incidence and Demographics of Soft Tissue Sarcomas
items.content: Soft tissue sarcomas are rare malignancies, constituting less than 1% of all cancers. They occur across all age groups but have a significant incidence in children (<15 years) at 15%, and adults (>55 years) at 40%. In the U.S., approximately 13,400 new cases were reported in 2023 with an annual age-adjusted incidence rate of 3 per 100,000 population. They account for 0.7% of all cancers in the general population but are more prevalent among children, making up 6.5% of pediatric cancers.
keyPoints: <1% of all malignancies
15% found in children <15 years
40% occur after age 55 years
13,400 new cases in the U.S. (2023)
Annual incidence: 3 per 100,000 population
0.7% of all cancers in general population
6.5% of all cancers in children
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases | SOFT TISSUE SARCOMAS | INCIDENCE | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases | SOFT TISSUE SARCOMAS | INCIDENCE | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Epidemiology and Etiologic Factors of Soft Tissue Sarcomas
items.content: Soft tissue sarcomas can arise from various etiologic factors. Environmental exposures such as trauma, chemical carcinogens (e.g., polycyclic hydrocarbons, asbestos), and radiation therapy are implicated in their development. Radiation-induced sarcomas typically occur within the irradiated field and risk increases over time. Viral associations include Kaposi's sarcoma linked to HHV-8, particularly in HIV-positive individuals. Immunologic factors such as congenital or acquired immunodeficiency also elevate sarcoma risk.
keyPoints: Malignant transformation from benign tumors is rare
Radiation therapy increases sarcoma risk within irradiated field over time
Kaposi's sarcoma linked to HHV-8 in HIV-positive patients
Immunodeficiency raises sarcoma risk
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases | SOFT TISSUE SARCOMAS | INCIDENCE | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Genetic Syndromes Associated with Sarcomas
items.content: Certain genetic syndromes predispose individuals to developing soft tissue sarcomas and other malignancies. Li-Fraumeni syndrome involves germline p53 mutations, increasing the risk of various cancers including osteosarcoma. Neurofibromatosis 1 (NF-1) can lead to malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumors due to neurofibromin dysfunction. Inherited retinoblastoma linked with RB1 gene mutations is associated with a higher incidence of osteosarcoma.
keyPoints: Li-Fraumeni syndrome involves germline p53 abnormalities and increased risk for sarcomas and other malignancies.
Neurofibromatosis 1 (NF-1) can lead to malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumors due to neurofibromin dysfunction.
RB1 gene mutations in inherited retinoblastoma are associated with osteosarcoma development.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | CLASSIFICATION

items.title: IGF-II Role in Sarcomas
items.content: Insulin-like growth factor II (IGF-II) is produced by some sarcomas and acts as an autocrine growth factor, promoting metastatic spread through different receptors. Excessive IGF-II secretion can lead to hypoglycemia.
keyPoints: IGF-II acts as an autocrine growth factor in some sarcomas.
IGF-II promotes motility and metastasis via distinct receptors.
Excess IGF-II may cause hypoglycemia.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | CLASSIFICATION

items.title: Sarcoma Classification and Translocations
items.content: Soft tissue sarcomas are classified based on differentiation patterns, with specific translocations providing diagnostic and prognostic insights. For example, 90% of synovial sarcomas have a t(X;18)(p11;q11) translocation involving SYT and SSX genes, affecting survival outcomes.
keyPoints: Sarcomas are classified by differentiation patterns towards normal tissue.
Approximately a third of soft tissue sarcomas exhibit diagnostic/translational relevance.
90% of synovial sarcomas have t(X;18)(p11;q11) translocation, impacting prognosis.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | CLASSIFICATION

items.title: When to Biopsy Soft Tissue Masses
items.content: All new and persistent or growing masses should be biopsied, either by a small incision or using a core-needle biopsy. This ensures that the biopsy site can be included in subsequent excisions without compromising definitive resection.
keyPoints: Biopsy is recommended for all new and persistent or growing masses.
Core-needle biopsy should be placed to allow inclusion in subsequent excision.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · DIAGNOSIS | STAGING AND PROGNOSIS

items.title: Patterns of Metastasis in Soft Tissue Sarcomas
items.content: The pulmonary parenchyma is the most common site for metastases, with exceptions including GISTs (liver), myxoid liposarcomas (fatty tissue), and clear cell sarcomas (bones). Lymph node metastases occur in 5% of cases, except in specific sarcomas where it can be as high as 17%. CNS metastases are rare except in alveolar soft part sarcoma.
keyPoints: Pulmonary parenchyma is the most common metastatic site.
Lymph node metastasis occurs in 5% of cases, up to 17% in certain sarcomas.
CNS metastases are rare except in alveolar soft part sarcoma.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · DIAGNOSIS | STAGING AND PROGNOSIS

items.title: Importance of Wide Excision for Soft Tissue Sarcomas
items.content: Wide excision with a negative margin is crucial in treating soft tissue sarcomas to prevent local recurrence. Marginal excision, or 'shelling out,' leads to high recurrence rates.
keyPoints: Soft tissue sarcomas grow along fascial planes and form pseudocapsules, misleadingly appearing well-encapsulated.
Marginal excision results in a 50–90% probability of local recurrence.
Wide excision with negative margins is standard for local disease control.
Adjuvant radiation therapy or chemotherapy can improve local control rates to 85–90%, comparable to radical excisions and amputations.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · Soft Tissue Sarcomas | SURGERY | RADIATION THERAPY

items.title: Role of Radiation Therapy in Soft Tissue Sarcoma Management
items.content: Radiation therapy is used as an adjuvant to surgery for improved local control. Preoperative radiation allows smaller fields and doses but increases wound complications, while postoperative radiation requires larger fields and higher doses due to hypoxia.
keyPoints: External-beam radiation therapy enhances limb-sparing surgery outcomes.
Preoperative radiation reduces field size and dose but raises wound complication rates.
Postoperative radiation needs larger fields and higher doses to address surgical bed hypoxia, increasing late complications.
Brachytherapy is effective (except in low-grade lesions), less time-consuming, and more cost-effective than external-beam therapy.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · Soft Tissue Sarcomas | SURGERY | RADIATION THERAPY

items.title: Chemotherapy Regimens for High-Risk Soft Tissue Sarcomas
items.content: Anthracycline and ifosfamide with growth factor support significantly improve overall survival in high-risk extremity soft tissue sarcomas.
keyPoints: Overall survival improved by 19% for high-risk (high-grade, ≥5 cm primary, or locally recurrent) extremity soft tissue sarcomas.
10-year survival reported at 61% with neoadjuvant use of anthracycline and ifosfamide combination.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · ADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY | ADVANCED DISEASE

items.title: Chemotherapy for Metastatic Soft Tissue Sarcomas
items.content: Doxorubicin and ifosfamide are the most active agents, with gemcitabine/docetaxel as a second-line regimen. Pazopanib is approved post-chemotherapy failure.
keyPoints: Up to 20% of patients achieve long-term survival after complete response.
Doxorubicin and ifosfamide show steep dose-response in sarcomas.
Pazopanib approved for advanced soft tissue sarcomas (excluding liposarcomas) post-chemotherapy failure.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · ADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY | ADVANCED DISEASE

items.title: Targeted Therapies and Immunotherapy in Sarcoma Management
items.content: Imatinib is standard for GISTs, with sunitinib and regorafenib as second- and third-line options. Atezolizumab shows efficacy in alveolar soft part sarcoma.
keyPoints: Three years of adjuvant imatinib superior to 1 year for high-risk GISTs.
Sunitinib and regorafenib approved for second- and third-line use in metastatic GIST.
Atezolizumab effective in about one-third of alveolar soft part sarcoma patients.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · ADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY | ADVANCED DISEASE

items.title: Incidence and Epidemiology of Bone Sarcomas
items.content: Bone sarcomas are significantly less common than soft tissue sarcomas, representing only 0.2% of all new malignancies in the United States with 3970 new cases reported in 2023.
keyPoints: Bone sarcomas account for 0.2% of all new malignancies.
Approximately 3970 new cases of bone sarcomas were reported in the US in 2023.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION | OSTEOSARCOMA

items.title: Classification and Common Types of Osteosarcoma
items.content: Osteosarcoma is the most common malignant bone tumor, accounting for nearly 45% of all bone sarcomas. It predominantly affects children and adolescents in their second decade of life.
keyPoints: Osteosarcoma accounts for almost 45% of all bone sarcomas.
~60% occur in children and adolescents during the second decade of life.
Males are affected 1.5–2 times more often than females.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION | OSTEOSARCOMA

items.title: Diagnostic Approach and Management of Osteosarcoma
items.content: Diagnosis of osteosarcoma involves clinical, radiologic, and pathologic features. Imaging such as CT scans and MRIs are crucial for assessing the extent of bone destruction and soft tissue involvement.
keyPoints: A plain radiograph reveals a destructive lesion with specific appearances like 'sunburst' and 'Codman's triangle'.
CT is preferred for defining bone destruction, MRI for intramedullary and soft tissue extension.
Standard management includes preoperative chemotherapy, limb-sparing surgery (>80% of patients), followed by postoperative chemotherapy.
Long-term survival rates range from 60 to 80%.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION | OSTEOSARCOMA

items.title: Chondrosarcoma: Indolent Nature and Surgical Management
items.content: Chondrosarcoma is a common adult bone sarcoma, often presenting with pain and swelling. It predominantly affects flat bones and long bone diaphyses. Radiographic features include lobular lesions with calcifications, but distinguishing low-grade from benign lesions can be challenging without clinical context. High-grade chondrosarcomas metastasize to the lungs, yet most are resistant to chemotherapy. Surgical resection remains the primary treatment, except for dedifferentiated and mesenchymal variants which respond to chemotherapy.
keyPoints: Constitutes ~20–25% of all bone sarcomas
Peak incidence in fourth to sixth decades
Predilection for flat bones (shoulder, pelvic girdles) and diaphyseal long bones
Radiographic features: lobular appearance with calcifications
Difficult to distinguish low-grade from benign lesions by x-ray or histology
New pain, inflammation signs, mass growth suggest malignancy
Most resistant to chemotherapy; surgical resection is primary treatment
Dedifferentiated and mesenchymal variants respond to chemotherapy
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · CHONDROSARCOMA | EWING'S SARCOMA

items.title: Ewing's Sarcoma: Aggressive Nature and Chemotherapy-Driven Treatment
items.content: Ewing's sarcoma primarily affects adolescents, with a peak in the second decade. It often involves long bone diaphyses or flat bones, showing an 'onion peel' periosteal reaction on radiographs. Histologically, it consists of small round blue cells and can be confused with other malignancies. The presence of p30/32 is diagnostic. Characterized by a t(11;22) translocation, Ewing's sarcoma is aggressive and systemic, commonly metastasizing to lungs, bones, and bone marrow. Systemic chemotherapy precedes local treatment like surgery or radiation, achieving 60–70% cure rates for localized disease.
keyPoints: Constitutes ~10–15% of all bone sarcomas
Peak incidence in the second decade
Typically involves diaphyseal long bones and flat bones
Radiographic feature: 'onion peel' periosteal reaction
Histology: small round blue cells, can mimic lymphoma or rhabdomyosarcoma
Diagnostic marker: p30/32 (mic-2 gene product)
Characteristic cytogenetic abnormality: t(11;22) translocation
Aggressive and systemic with common metastases to lung, bones, bone marrow
Systemic chemotherapy is primary treatment, often before surgery
Curative rates of 60–70% for localized tumors
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · CHONDROSARCOMA | EWING'S SARCOMA

items.title: Common Sites and Symptoms of Bone Metastases
items.content: Bone metastases are more prevalent than primary bone tumors, with prostate, breast, and lung cancers accounting for 80% of cases. They typically spread hematogenously but can also invade locally from soft tissue masses. The most frequent sites affected include the vertebrae, proximal femur, pelvis, ribs, sternum, proximal humerus, and skull. Symptoms often include pain, swelling, nerve root or spinal cord compression, pathologic fractures, myelophthisis, and hypercalcemia due to bony destruction.
keyPoints: Prostate, breast, and lung cancers account for 80% of bone metastases.
Common sites: vertebrae, proximal femur, pelvis, ribs, sternum, proximal humerus, skull.
Symptoms include pain (often worse at night), swelling, nerve root/spinal cord compression, pathologic fractures, myelophthisis, hypercalcemia.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · TUMORS METASTATIC TO BONE

items.title: Detection and Characteristics of Osteolytic vs. Osteoblastic Lesions
items.content: Bone metastases can lead to osteolysis or osteogenesis, with some tumors producing both types of lesions. Osteolytic lesions are best detected by plain radiography when >1 cm in size and may cause hypercalcemia. They result from substances that induce bone resorption or cytokines promoting osteoclast formation. Osteoblastic lesions, detectable via radionuclide bone scanning due to new bone formation, often show increased bone density on radiographs and are associated with higher serum alkaline phosphatase levels. These lesions can lead to hypocalcemia if extensive.
keyPoints: Osteolytic lesions: detected by plain radiography (>1 cm), associated with hypercalcemia.
Osteoblastic lesions: detectable by radionuclide bone scanning, show increased bone density, higher serum alkaline phosphatase levels, may cause hypocalcemia.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · TUMORS METASTATIC TO BONE

items.title: Treatment Approaches for Metastatic Bone Disease
items.content: The treatment strategy for metastatic bone disease varies based on the underlying malignancy and symptomatology, with some tumors being curable while others are managed palliatively. Pain relief can be achieved through local radiation therapy or systemic treatments like hormone inhibition for hormonally responsive tumors.
keyPoints: Curability depends on the type of cancer (e.g., lymphoma, Hodgkin's lymphoma).
Pain management options include local radiation and bone-seeking radionuclides such as Strontium-89, Samarium-153, and Radium-223.
Hormone inhibition is effective for tumors responsive to hormones (antiandrogens for prostate cancer, antiestrogens for breast cancer).
Denosumab inhibits osteoclastic activity and increases bone mineral density.
Bisphosphonates like pamidronate relieve pain and inhibit bone resorption, reducing fracture risk in osteolytic metastases from breast cancer and multiple myeloma.
Monthly administration of bisphosphonates prevents bone-related clinical events and may reduce incidence of bone metastases in women with breast cancer.
Prophylactic internal fixation is indicated for weight-bearing bones threatened by refractory metastatic lesions.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · Metastatic Bone Disease

items.title: Management of Bone Pain and Hypercalcemia at End-of-Life Care
items.content: Bone pain, especially prevalent at the end of life in patients with metastatic bone disease, requires an adequate pain relief regimen including sufficient narcotic analgesics. Additionally, hypercalcemia management is critical for these patients.
keyPoints: Bone pain is common at the end of life and necessitates a comprehensive pain relief plan with adequate narcotics.
Hypercalcemia management details are covered in Chapter 422.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · Metastatic Bone Disease

items.question: Which surgical approach is the standard procedure for local disease of soft tissue sarcomas?
options: Wide excision with a negative margin incorporating the biopsy site
items.explanation: Wide excision with a negative margin, including the biopsy site, is standard to prevent local recurrence.

items.subtopic: Soft Tissue Sarcomas | SURGERY | RADIATION THERAPY
items.question: What is the main disadvantage of preoperative radiation therapy in soft tissue sarcoma treatment?
options: Requires larger fields and higher doses
Results in a higher rate of wound complications
Is less time consuming than postoperative therapy
items.explanation: Preoperative radiation allows smaller fields but results in a higher rate of wound complications.

items.subtopic: Soft Tissue Sarcomas | SURGERY | RADIATION THERAPY
items.question: Under what condition is limb-sparing surgery not indicated for soft tissue sarcoma?
options: When neurovascular structures are involved with serious functional consequences
items.explanation: Limb-sparing surgery is not indicated when resection involves neurovascular structures causing serious limb dysfunction.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · Soft Tissue Sarcomas | SURGERY | RADIATION THERAPY

items.statement: UPS is considered part of the spectrum of osteosarcoma.
items.explanation: UPS (Undifferentiated Pleomorphic Sarcoma) is managed similarly to osteosarcoma.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION | OSTEOSARCOMA

items.statement: Most chondrosarcomas are responsive to chemotherapy.
items.explanation: Most chondrosarcomas are resistant to chemotherapy, with surgical resection being the mainstay of therapy.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · CHONDROSARCOMA | EWING'S SARCOMA

items.statement: The classic cytogenetic abnormality associated with Ewing's sarcoma is a translocation between chromosomes 11 and 22.
items.explanation: Ewing's sarcoma is characterized by the reciprocal translocation t(11;22), creating a chimeric gene product.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · CHONDROSARCOMA | EWING'S SARCOMA

items.statement: Osteolytic lesions are more commonly associated with hypocalcemia.
items.explanation: Osteolytic lesions are associated with hypercalcemia, not hypocalcemia.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · TUMORS METASTATIC TO BONE

items.statement: Prostate cancer primarily produces osteoblastic lesions.
items.explanation: Prostate cancer is known to produce mainly osteoblastic lesions.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · TUMORS METASTATIC TO BONE

items.statement: Overall survival in metastatic bone disease is independent of the prognosis of the underlying tumor.
items.explanation: Overall survival is related to the prognosis of the underlying tumor.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · Metastatic Bone Disease

items.statement: Bisphosphonates may reduce the incidence of bone metastases in women with breast cancer.
items.explanation: Monthly administration of bisphosphonates may prevent bone-related clinical events and reduce the incidence of bone metastases.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · Metastatic Bone Disease

items.assertion: The RB1 locus mutation is associated with osteosarcoma development in patients who survive retinoblastoma.
items.reason: Germline mutation of the RB1 locus (chromosome 13q14) in inherited retinoblastoma is linked to osteosarcoma.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | CLASSIFICATION

items.assertion: IGF-II acts solely through IGF-I receptors.
items.reason: IGF-II stimulates growth through IGF-I receptors but affects motility through different receptors.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because IGF-II also acts through other receptors for motility.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · GENETIC CONSIDERATIONS | CLASSIFICATION

items.subtopic: Soft Tissue Sarcomas | SURGERY | RADIATION THERAPY
items.assertion: Brachytherapy is less time consuming and less expensive than external-beam radiation therapy.
items.reason: Brachytherapy involves inserting the radiation source into the tumor bed, which is comparable in efficacy except for low-grade lesions.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason explains why brachytherapy is less time consuming and less expensive.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · Soft Tissue Sarcomas | SURGERY | RADIATION THERAPY

items.assertion: Trabectedin is approved for use in advanced leiomyosarcomas and liposarcomas.
items.reason: It resulted in significant improvement in progression-free survival compared to dacarbazine.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · ADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY | ADVANCED DISEASE

items.assertion: Atezolizumab is effective in most forms of sarcoma.
items.reason: It has produced responses in about one-third of patients with alveolar soft part sarcoma.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because immune checkpoint inhibitors have generally been ineffective in most forms of sarcoma, though atezolizumab has shown some efficacy in a specific subtype.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · ADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY | ADVANCED DISEASE

items.assertion: Osteosarcoma has a predilection for the metaphyses of long bones.
items.reason: The most common sites are the distal femur, proximal tibia, and proximal humerus.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION | OSTEOSARCOMA

items.assertion: Radiation therapy is a standard treatment for osteosarcoma.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true; radiation therapy has no role in routine management due to radioresistance.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY | CLASSIFICATION | OSTEOSARCOMA

items.assertion: Pain in metastatic bone disease can be relieved by local radiation therapy.
items.reason: Local radiation therapy is effective for pain relief in some metastatic bone tumors.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Soft Tissue and Bone Sarcomas and Bone Metastases · Metastatic Bone Disease
